title: Feedbacks upon feedbacks: Rock weathering and the climate
gist: A new Nature Communications study uses rhenium isotopes to show that oxidation of organic carbon in eroding rocks amplified CO2 emissions during the Toarcian warming event 183 million years ago, filling a previously unexplained gap in volcanic CO2 emissions. The findings suggest this feedback mechanism may also be active today, slightly eroding humanity's remaining carbon budget over the coming centuries. Scientists disagree on the magnitude of the effect and how human-driven land use changes may accelerate or dampen it.

themes.theme: Organic Carbon Weathering as a Climate Feedback

insights.claim: Erosion of organic-carbon-rich sediments releases CO2 to the atmosphere, potentially acting as a significant amplifying feedback to warming events rather than only a carbon sink as previously assumed.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Positive carbon-cycle feedbacks from geologic organic carbon weathering could reduce the effective carbon budget for limiting warming, relevant to net-zero planning.
insights.quote: Weathering breakdown of rocks can actually be acting as a carbon dioxide source, and this can be quite large.
insights.locator: Mind the gap section

insights.claim: During the Toarcian warming event (183 million years ago), organic carbon weathering intensified as temperatures rose, indicating a temperature-sensitive positive feedback loop between warming and CO2 release from eroding sediments.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: A self-reinforcing warming feedback driven by rock organic carbon is empirically confirmed in the geological record, suggesting analogous dynamics are possible under modern climate change.
insights.quote: Our modern measurements of this process suggest if you warm up the planet, you should weather this material more intensely, and that's exactly what we see in the core as we had this warming event.
insights.locator: Mind the gap section

insights.claim: Organic carbon weathering provided sufficient CO2 to fill the gap left by volcanic emissions alone in the Toarcian event, resolving why volcanic CO2 was insufficient to account for observed warming.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Confirms organic carbon weathering as a material amplifier in past warming events, strengthening the case that current models undercount positive carbon feedbacks.
insights.quote: Lo and behold, we end up finding that we can explain this apparent discrepancy.
insights.locator: Mind the gap section

insights.claim: Organic carbon weathering is expected to add CO2 to the atmosphere over the next few centuries at a low but sustained rate, eating into humanity's remaining carbon budget without constituting an immediate catastrophe.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Long-term carbon budgets used for climate policy may be overstated if this feedback is not accounted for, implying tighter emission reduction targets.
insights.quote: It's not disaster stations, but it is an amount of carbon that could be released at a rate more than it is right now, and that does eat into our carbon budget.
insights.locator: A small, long-term uncertain impact for humans

themes.theme: Rhenium Isotopes as a Novel Geological Tracer

insights.claim: Rhenium isotope ratios in seabed sediments serve as a novel quantitative tracer for organic carbon oxidation on land, with the ratio of rhenium-187 to rhenium-185 directly reflecting the intensity of organic carbon weathering at a given time.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: This analytical method opens a new measurement capability for studying carbon-cycle feedbacks in ancient and future warming events, with potential applications in climate modeling.
insights.quote: This is a really important paper because it is one of the first to use rhenium isotopes in a past geologic context, and that really opens up this system to be able to study further in the past.
insights.locator: Sampling the air using sediments from the seabed

insights.claim: Rhenium concentrations in rocks are as low as one billionth of a gram per gram of rock, and recent advances in mass spectrometer sensitivity have been a 'game changer' enabling this type of study for the first time.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Instrumentation advances (ICP-MS sensitivity) are unlocking previously inaccessible geochemical signals, accelerating climate science progress.
insights.quote: Recent improvements in the sensitivity of mass spectrometers have been 'a bit of a game changer' in allowing studies like this.
insights.locator: Sampling the air using sediments from the seabed

insights.claim: The study used a 1,300-meter rock core drilled in Wales in the 1960s and now stored by the British Geological Survey, demonstrating that archival geological samples are a critical resource for modern climate science.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Archival geological cores hold untapped climate data accessible with modern instrumentation, representing a low-cost path to improved carbon-cycle understanding.
insights.locator: Sampling the air using sediments from the seabed

themes.theme: Earth's Silicate Weathering Thermostat Under Stress

insights.claim: The silicate weathering negative feedback (Earth's CO2 thermostat) remains intact but must work harder to compensate for added CO2 from organic carbon weathering, meaning it is not overridden but is under greater stress.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Earth's long-run stabilization mechanism remains functional, but the additional positive feedback from organic carbon weathering implies slower recovery from warming events, extending timescales of climate risk.
insights.quote: Silicate weathering is still playing a major role. We're not challenging that. It means that silicate weathering has to work harder.
insights.locator: Where does that leave Earth's thermostat

insights.claim: The silicate weathering feedback is ultimately stronger than organic carbon weathering because silicate mineral volumes available for CO2 drawdown are much larger than the finite supply of organic-carbon-rich sediments.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Earth will not enter runaway warming from this feedback alone, but the transition period before silicate weathering compensates could span centuries to millennia.
insights.quote: That's why that feedback ultimately is a stronger one—we have a much bigger buffer to play with.
insights.locator: Where does that leave Earth's thermostat

insights.claim: The organic carbon weathering feedback has a built-in ceiling: once available organic-carbon-rich sediments exposed on land are exhausted, the positive feedback terminates, placing a hard limit on its total CO2 contribution.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: The feedback is self-limiting over geological timescales, reducing systemic tail risk but the timing of exhaustion is unknown and could span very long periods.
insights.quote: At some point, you're going to run out of organic carbon to oxidize, and that's then going to place a hard limit on the strength of this feedback.
insights.locator: Where does that leave Earth's thermostat

themes.theme: Uncertainty and Skepticism About Feedback Magnitude

insights.claim: A co-external reviewer (Jeremy Caves Rugenstein of Colorado State University) is skeptical that the estimated CO2 flux from organic carbon weathering is as large as the study claims, calling the figures 'enormous' and 'difficult to believe.'
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Uncertainty about magnitude means the carbon budget impact of this feedback could be significantly overstated; investors in carbon markets or carbon removal technologies should not over-weight this as a near-term risk.
insights.quote: Their estimates of the total amount of carbon delivered by this feedback are enormous. I find it difficult to believe that these carbon fluxes are going to be as big as they think they are.
insights.locator: Mind the gap section

insights.claim: Permafrost and soil CO2 emissions represent a larger and more immediate positive feedback than geologic organic carbon weathering, which operates at lower rates over longer timescales.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Near-term climate risk prioritization should focus on permafrost thaw and soil carbon loss rather than geologic organic carbon weathering.
insights.quote: That's going to be a bigger positive feedback than this geologic organic carbon will be—this could be sustained for longer, but at much lower rates.
insights.locator: A small, long-term uncertain impact for humans

themes.theme: Human Land Use and Sedimentation Complicating the Feedback

insights.claim: Human-driven changes to sedimentation patterns—including large-scale sediment movement—could have either accelerated or decelerated organic carbon burial, creating uncertainty about whether anthropogenic land use amplifies or dampens the organic carbon weathering feedback.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: The net effect of agriculture, mining, and infrastructure construction on the organic carbon cycle is unknown, adding a hidden variable to carbon accounting for land use sectors.
insights.quote: We have moved so much sediment around as humans and changed sedimentation patterns that it's just difficult to know.
insights.locator: A small, long-term uncertain impact for humans

insights.claim: More extreme storms driving debris flows, landslides, and increased erosion under climate change may enhance organic carbon oxidation, but could equally shuttle organic carbon to continental shelves where it gets buried and sequestered rather than oxidized.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: The direction of the climate-erosion-carbon feedback under intensifying weather extremes is unresolved, leaving net carbon cycle impact ambiguous for near-term policy.
insights.quote: We have more extreme storms driving more debris flows, more landslides, and more erosion. Does that tend to enhance organic carbon oxidation, or does it actually dampen it because you end up shuttling all this organic carbon… to the shelf where it just gets buried?
insights.locator: A small, long-term uncertain impact for humans

insights.claim: Fully understanding the CO2 mass balance requires mapping the global distribution of organic-rich sediments currently eroding and how temperature and rainfall changes affect those specific locations.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Current Earth system models likely lack sufficient spatial resolution on organic carbon-rich sediment exposure to accurately project this feedback, suggesting an underappreciated modeling gap.
insights.quote: We really have to understand the more complicated organic carbon system to be able to fully balance out CO2 mass balance.
insights.locator: A small, long-term uncertain impact for humans

themes.theme: Toarcian Event as Analog for Modern Climate Change

insights.claim: The Toarcian Ocean Anoxic Event 183 million years ago produced 6-7 degrees Celsius of global warming, a mass extinction of marine species, and reshuffled land species, making it one of the best-studied analogs for large-scale volcanic-driven climate change.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: The Toarcian provides an empirical benchmark for ecosystem response to rapid warming at magnitudes now considered plausible under high-emission scenarios, useful for scenario planning.
insights.locator: Sampling the air using sediments from the seabed

insights.claim: Past global warming events associated with Large Igneous Provinces systematically buried large quantities of organic matter at sea, producing black shales; when subsequently uplifted and eroded, these formations become source rocks for CO2 release in future warming events.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Past mass extinction events created the very organic carbon reservoirs now at risk of oxidation, establishing a long-cycle link between ancient volcanism and modern climate feedbacks.
insights.locator: Sampling the air using sediments from the seabed

insights.claim: The research group of Bob Hilton at Oxford has been credited with revolutionizing scientific understanding of how geologic organic carbon interacts with climate, with this study being a further milestone in that body of work.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Oxford's Earth Sciences group is a key node for carbon-cycle research with policy implications; their future findings will likely shape climate modeling standards.
insights.quote: This group… has revolutionized our understanding of how geologic organic carbon interacts with climate.
insights.locator: Sampling the air using sediments from the seabed

themes.theme: Implications for Carbon Budgets and Climate Policy

insights.claim: Organic carbon weathering represents an additional CO2 source that erodes the effective remaining carbon budget for 1.5°C or 2°C targets, though at rates lower than permafrost thaw and over longer timescales.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Official carbon budgets (IPCC and national targets) may be somewhat optimistic if geologic organic carbon feedbacks are excluded, adding to already tight headroom for emissions.
insights.quote: it is an amount of carbon that could be released at a rate more than it is right now, and that does eat into our carbon budget.
insights.locator: A small, long-term uncertain impact for humans

insights.claim: The study published in Nature Communications (DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-71533-6) provides the first geological-record confirmation of organic carbon weathering as a quantified CO2 feedback amplifier, opening a new area of empirical constraint for Earth system models.
insights.speaker: Howard Lee
insights.so_what: Earth system models used for IPCC projections have a new empirically validated feedback mechanism to incorporate, potentially tightening uncertainty bounds on long-term warming trajectories.
insights.locator: Nature Communications citation
